author: @Modikie
text:
the thing is with android vs iPhone debate, its really putting apple in a disadvantageous position because though they are comparable in terms of marketshare, at the end of the way, you are still choosing between what one company can offer, vs what many, many different companies can offer. So in that way with this kind of system, its hard for the iPhone to win because it is competing with different android phones, with different strengths and weaknesses, under one umbrella which leads to the android usually coming out onto because some android phones are better, even if the experience on the iPhone is better in most cases. for example with features. yes, android is going to have more features because as a whole, you have many more companies doing many more things. same with customization. You have one OS, for one company, vs a platform which must be a one size fits all situation that can be used by anybody, which automatically means more ability to do things differently. so ofc android will be better in this way ultimately, even if the experience of that customization, is worse. 

Apps, I have always known apps to be better on the iPhone. there are not as many apps in some cases, but the apps that are on iOS, are all high quality, and are very, very unlikely to be taken down from the App Store for doing shady practices. It depends on preference but still, the app experience is still better on iOS for all the apps people regularly use on their phone. even on my 6 year old iPhone 12 mini, Spotify opened up just a slight tad bit slower than my s23 plus (a 3 year newer device), but when it came to USING the app, the iPhone was hands down the better experience because everything stays loaded, and switching between menu's is flawlessly smooth, while on my s23 plus, it would often hang for a moment on a blank screen before it brings the content in. i dont care if it opens the app slightly quicker, what matters is using the app, and at the end of the day, the iPhone just does it better.
